title: Psalms 145
subtitle: I Will Exalt You, My God the King

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — “Great Is the LORD and Most Worthy of Praise”

heb.paragraph: The ONLY psalm titled təhillâ (“praise”) — from which the entire book gets its Hebrew name Təhillîm (“Praises”). David’s final acrostic. "Great is the LORD and most worthy of praise; his greatness no one can fathom" (v.3). "The LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and rich in love. The LORD is good to all; he has compassion on all he has made" (vv.8–9). The Exodus 34:6 formula for the last time in the Psalter. "Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations" (v.13).

notes.note: "I will exalt you, my God the King. Every day I will praise you. Great is the LORD and most worthy of praise; his greatness no one can fathom." Daily, perpetual, unfathomable praise. The psalm that gives the book its name.

"The LORD is gracious and compassionate, slow to anger and rich in love. The LORD is good to ALL; he has compassion on ALL he has made." The Exodus 34:6 formula’s final Psalter appearance. Two “alls”: good to ALL, compassion on ALL. Universal goodness.

"Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations." The kingdom that Daniel prophesied (Dan 2:44; 7:14). Everlasting — no expiration date, no succession crisis.

refs.note: At the name of Jesus every knee will bow—universal exaltation.
The living creatures praise God's greatness day and night without ceasing.
Teach these words to your children—one generation commending God's works to the next.

hist.context: Psalm 145 is David's last titled psalm and the only Psalm labeled tehillah (praise). It is an acrostic, each verse beginning with successive Hebrew letters. The psalm celebrates YHWH's greatness, mighty acts, and compassion—themes to be passed from generation to generation. Jewish tradition recites Psalm 145 three times daily. It serves as gateway to the Final Hallel (146-150).

sections.header: Verses 14–21 — “The LORD Is Near to All Who Call on Him”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD is near (qārōb) to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth" (v.18). God upholds the falling, lifts the bowed down (v.14). He opens his hand and satisfies every living thing (v.16). He is righteous in all his ways and faithful in all he does (v.17). He fulfils the desires of those who fear him (v.19). He watches over all who love him (v.20). "My mouth will speak in praise of the LORD. Let every creature praise his holy name for ever and ever" (v.21). David’s last psalm-voice: let EVERY CREATURE praise. The Psalter begins to expand toward its universal finale.

notes.note: "The LORD upholds all who fall and lifts up all who are bowed down. The eyes of all look to you. You open your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing." Three universals: upholds all who fall, eyes of all look, satisfies every living thing. God’s open hand is the Psalter’s image of daily provision.

"The LORD is near to all who call on him in truth. He fulfils the desires of those who fear him; he hears their cry and saves them." Nearness + fulfilment + hearing + saving. The four-fold response to those who call.

"Let EVERY CREATURE praise his holy name for ever and ever." The Psalter’s final David-psalm ends with the widest possible summons: every creature, holy name, forever. The stage is set for Psalms 146–150.

refs.note: God feeds the birds of the air—the Father provides for his creatures.
God provides rain and crops, filling hearts with food and gladness.
See what great love the Father has lavished on us—near to all who call.

hist.context: The second half emphasizes YHWH's providence: 'The eyes of all look to you, and you give them their food at the proper time.' God is near to all who call on him in truth, fulfilling desires, saving, and watching over those who love him. The psalm concludes with universal praise: 'My mouth will speak the praise of the LORD. Let every creature praise his holy name.' This sets up the Psalter's grand finale.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThey celebrate your abundant goodness and joyfully sing of your righteousness.ESVThey shall pour forth the fame of your abundant goodness and shall sing aloud of your righteousness.NIVThey speak of the glorious splendor of your majesty— and I will meditate on your wonderful works.ESVOn the glorious splendor of your majesty, and on your wondrous works, I will meditate.

rows.label: Near and Open-Handed — 145:14–21
rows.text: "He OPENS HIS HAND." "The LORD is NEAR to all who call." "Let EVERY CREATURE praise." The Psalter begins its ascent.

hebtext.note: The ONLY psalm titled təhillâ (“praise”) — from which the entire book gets its Hebrew name Təhillîm (“Praises”). David’s final acrostic. "Your kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and your dominion endures through all generations" (v.13).

"The LORD is near (qārōb) to all who call on him, to all who call on him in truth" (v.18). God upholds the falling, lifts the bowed down (v.14). themes.note: Worship peaks at 10: THE psalm of praise — the one that names the book. Sovereignty at 9: everlasting kingdom. Mercy at 9: Exodus 34:6’s final appearance + universal compassion.
